does: This paper develops a security framework for multi-agent GIS systems that combines risk identification, red-teaming evaluation, and prompt hardening. The authors test their approach on a commercial geospatial system using a modular state-machine orchestration architecture that decomposes agent behaviors into reusable components. They evaluate system robustness through multi-turn adversarial attacks using an adaptive attacker LLM paired with a deterministic judge providing binary outcomes and rationales. The core methodological contribution is a prompt optimization framework that treats prompts as structured signatures and incorporates adversarial demonstrations to systematically improve security resilience while maintaining task performance—addressing the tension between robustness and functionality in agentic systems.
connects: This work extends prior research on adversarial robustness of language models and agentic systems by focusing specifically on the GIS domain where spatial reasoning and tool use create distinct attack surfaces. It builds on existing red-teaming methodologies for LLM evaluation but adapts them for multi-agent coordination scenarios requiring sequential decision-making and tool orchestration. The prompt optimization approach connects to earlier work on prompt engineering and adversarial training for language models, translating those techniques into the context of complex agent workflows. The modular orchestration framework responds to challenges in agent composability and interpretability documented in recent agentic AI research, while the structured signature approach to prompts relates to ongoing efforts to formalize and systematize prompt design beyond ad-hoc engineering.
enables: This framework enables development of security evaluation standards for agentic systems across domains beyond GIS, establishing a template for risk assessment in systems combining language models with specialized tools and spatial reasoning. The red-teaming methodology with adaptive attackers and deterministic judging could generalize to other high-stakes agentic applications requiring robustness verification. The prompt hardening approach using adversarial demonstrations suggests future work on certified robustness guarantees for agentic systems and systematic defenses against multi-turn attacks. The modular orchestration abstraction points toward building composable, auditable agent architectures where security properties can be verified at the component level, potentially enabling safer deployment of multi-agent systems in critical infrastructure and decision-support contexts where GIS analysis informs real-world planning.
